plainMeaning: In the seventh vow a Jain layperson limits what he eats and uses, so here he confesses eating any of the twenty-two forbidden foods, the thirty-two many-souled root vegetables, stale or living food, and eating after dark.
recitation: sātamuṁ bhogopabhoga-vrata dvividha. bhojanataḥ karmataśca. tatra bhojanataḥ iṇe vrate — pañcuṁbari mahaviga-i, hima visa karage ya savva maṭṭī ya. rāi-bhoyaṇāgaṁ ciya, bahubīya aṇaṁta saṁdhāṇaṁ ||1|| gholavaḍāṁ vāyaṁgaṇa, amuṇiya nāmāṇi phulla phalayāṇi. tucchaphalaṁ caliyarasaṁ, vajjaya abhujjāiṁ bāvīsaṁ ||2|| e bāvīsa abhakṣya. savvāo kandajāi, sūraṇakando ya vajjakando ya. ada-haliddā ya tahā, adaṁ taha alla-kaccūro ||1|| sattāvarī virālī, kuṁāri taha thohari galoi ya. lhasaṇa vaṁsa-karillā, gajjara taha luṇāo loḍho ||2|| giri-kaṇiṇa kisala-patto, khīri-suā thega alla-mutthā ya. taha loṇa rūkkha-challī, khīlluhaḍo amaya-vallī ya ||3|| mūlā taha bhūmi-ruhā, virūhāi ḍhakka vatthulo paḍhamo. sūara vallo ya tahā, pallaṁko komalaṁ biliyā ||4|| ālū taha piṁḍālu, battīsaṁ jāṇiūṇā huṁtāiṁ. iyāiṁ buddhimayā, parihariyavvā payatteṇaṁ ||5|| e batrīsa anantakāya yathāśakte roga durbhikṣādika mūkī pariharuṁ. e sātamā bhogopabhoga (bhojana) vratataṇā pāñca atichāra śodhuṁ. sacitta āhāre, sacitta paḍibaddha āhāre, appoṣahi bhakṣaṇayā, duppoṣahi bhakṣaṇayā, tucchoṣahi bhakṣaṇayā. sacittataṇo niyama līdhe ajāṇatāṁ adhikuṁ sacitta līdhuṁ hoya. sacitta pratibaddha — rāyaṇa muha ghālī nikālī hoya; vṛkṣataṇo guṁda ukheḍī tatkāla acitta-buddhe āhāryo hoya. appoṣadhi — polī māṁhelo kaṇa bhakhyo hoya. duppoṣadhi — olā, pahoṁka acitta-buddhe āhāryo hoya. tucchoṣadhi — kuṁalī āṁbalī, kuṁalāṁ vāgaraḍā, mohari, kuṁpalī, sūkṣma kerī taṇuṁ bhakṣaṇa kīdhuṁ hoya. ane rātre bhojana kīdhuṁ hoya. lagabhaga velāe jimyā hoie. jimavā aṁdhāre beṭhā hoie. gulale pāṇī pīdhuṁ hoya. 'sacitta-davva-vigai' e caudda niyama divasagata, rātrigata ciṁtavyā na hoya. varṣākāle jūnuṁ ṭoparuṁ, khāreka, khajura āhāryā hoya. āmaṇābora, jāṁbu, pīlū, ākhī vālholī līdhī hoya. pahoṁka svahaste kīdhā hoya. kācuṁ lūṇa, vāśī vaḍāṁ, vāśī polī, soḷa pahora uparāṁta dahīṁ vāvaryuṁ hoya. saṁsakta phūla phala upajīvyāṁ hoya, aṇagaḷa pāṇī pīdhuṁ hoya. aneruṁ e sātamā bhogopabhoga-vrata viṣe.

wordByWord.notes: Numbers the vow: the third guṇavrata, seventh of the twelve.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bhoga 'a thing enjoyed once (food)' + upabhoga 'a thing used repeatedly (clothes, dwelling)' + vrata 'vow'.
wordByWord.notes: The vow that caps both single-use and recurring consumption.
The split announced immediately as bhojanataḥ / karmataḥ.
The aspect treated in this verse; the karmataḥ aspect (15 karmādāna) follows in verse 8.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. karman 'action, livelihood' + -taḥ + ca 'and' (sandhi karmataḥ + ca).
wordByWord.notes: Names but defers the second aspect.
Picks out the food-aspect for treatment.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa locative of demonstrative (Skt. asmin).

wordByWord.notes: 'In this vow' introduces the abhakṣya gāthās.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pañca 'five' + udumbara, the Ficus group (umbara, vaṭa, pippala, plakṣa, kākodumbara).
wordByWord.notes: Forbidden because their fruit teems with minute living beings.

wordByWord.gloss: the great vikṛtis (four most degrading rich foods)
wordByWord.notes: The four mahāvigai: madya (liquor), māṁsa (meat), madhu (honey), navanīta (butter).
Forbidden as a body of countless ap-kāya (water-souls).
Lethal substance, taken as inedible.
Like ice, a mass of water-bodied life.
Recurs throughout the gāthās as connective; glossed once.
Qualifies 'all earth/clay'.
Eating earth is forbidden — pṛthvī-kāya (earth-bodied) life.
Rātri-bhojana, a cardinal Jain abhakṣya.
Emphatic filler.
Each seed a separate one-sensed life; e.g. figs, certain gourds.
Forward-points to the 32-fold list below.
Preserves that breed life after a set time.
Becomes a breeding ground for life once soaked and kept.
Abhakṣya for its many seeds and reputed afflictions.
Qualifies fruits/flowers whose name one does not know — avoid them.
'(Of) unknown names' — un-named flowers and fruits.
Pairs with phulla: unknown flowers and fruits.
Fruit with much to discard and little to eat — disproportionate harm.
Fermented or off-tasting food, now full of life.
The operative command of the gāthā.
The 22 inedibles as a class.
Caps the abhakṣya count.
Recurs to close each list.
Glossary term; closing label of the abhakṣya list.
Begins the 32 anantakāya; 'all kinds of tuber'.
Sweeping head-term: every bulbous root.
Identifications of several items below vary by edition; see notes.
Exact botanical identity uncertain.
List-connective; recurs (taha) throughout, glossed once.
Dry ginger (śuṇṭhī) is permitted; fresh is anantakāya.
alla = moist/green recurs below.
Likely vidārī-kanda; identity approximate.
Fleshy spurge.
Classic anantakāya/abhakṣya.
Uncertain identification.

wordByWord.etymology: Uncertain; possibly Symplocos (lodhra) or a root.
wordByWord.notes: Identity uncertain.
Identity approximate.
All first-budding leaf-sprouts are anantakāya.
Possibly a milk-exuding tuber/herb.
Identity uncertain.
Distinct from kācuṁ lūṇa (raw salt) in the prose; identity approximate.
Fresh bark holds vegetal life.
Identity uncertain.
Root, leaf, flower and pod of radish are all anantakāya.
Mushroom/fungus, prototypical anantakāya.
Sprouted pulses and grains.
Uncertain.
Only the first/young growth (paḍhamo) is anantakāya.
Qualifies vatthulo: its earliest sprouting stage.
Reads with vallo as sūkara-vallī; uncertain.
Identity approximate.
Qualifies biliyā: the soft new bilva shoot.
In older texts ālu denotes a yam; broadly any starchy tuber.
Closes the enumerated items.
Fixes the anantakāya count, though several names are obscure.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit hunta (pres. part. of bhū 'to be').
wordByWord.notes: 'Knowing these thirty-two to be (anantakāya)'.
The verse appeals to discernment, not mere rule-following.
The injunctive verb of the gāthā.
Renunciation must be deliberate, not casual.
Closing tally in Gujarati.
Glossary term; the list's label.
Introduces the householder's realistic exception clause.
A circumstance permitting medicinal exception.
'Excepting illness, famine, etc., I renounce these.'
The reciter's personal vow.
Recurring in the purification formula 'e … vratataṇā pāñca atichāra śodhuṁ'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. aticāra; spelled atiyāra elsewhere in this book.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term.
Opens the atichāra review.
Sacittāhāra — eating un-rendered, still-living matter.

wordByWord.gloss: 2nd atichāra: eating food attached to a living thing
wordByWord.notes: Food still joined to a living stalk/tree, e.g. fruit on the stone.
Glossed in the prose as the inner grain of a half-baked polī.
E.g. parched ola/pohã eaten as if rendered lifeless.

wordByWord.gloss: 5th atichāra: eating pithless food with much waste
wordByWord.notes: Unripe tamarind, tender mango etc.: great harm, little nourishment.

wordByWord.gloss: having taken a rule limiting living food
wordByWord.notes: Sets up the first confessed lapse.
Part of the known/unknown exhaustiveness pairing.
Took more sacitta than the rule allowed.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati optative — 'it may have been done'.
wordByWord.notes: The recurring confessional auxiliary throughout the prose.
Example of sacitta-pratibaddha: pulp put in the mouth, stone taken out.

wordByWord.gloss: having put in the mouth and taken out (the stone)
wordByWord.notes: Eating pulp around a still-living seed.

wordByWord.gloss: having scraped gum off a tree
wordByWord.notes: Fresh gum, still living, eaten.

wordByWord.gloss: under the impression it was lifeless
wordByWord.notes: Mistaking still-sentient matter for rendered/dead.
Confessional auxiliary, like līdhuṁ hoya.
Concrete gloss of appoṣadhi (half-cooked).
Example of duppoṣadhi.
Recurs below as a self-made item.
Example of tucchoṣadhi.
Identity approximate.
Much pit, little flesh — pithless waste.
Rātri-bhojana again, now as a confessed lapse.

wordByWord.gloss: may have eaten at twilight (uncertain time)
wordByWord.notes: Eating when day/night boundary is doubtful.

wordByWord.gloss: may have sat to eat in the dark
wordByWord.notes: Cannot inspect food for life in darkness.
Reading approximate; drinking without inspection.

wordByWord.gloss: the fourteen daily rules (beginning 'sacitta, dravya, vigai…')
wordByWord.notes: The 14 niyama a śrāvaka resolves each day/night (food-items, directions, etc.).

wordByWord.gloss: may not have contemplated (them) for day and for night
wordByWord.etymology: divasa 'day' + rātri 'night' + gata 'pertaining to' + ciṁtavavuṁ 'to reflect'.
wordByWord.notes: Failing to fix the fourteen rules each period.
Season when produce most readily breeds life.
Prone to worms in monsoon.
Identity approximate.
Multi-seeded pods full of life.

wordByWord.gloss: may have made (parched grain) with one's own hand
wordByWord.notes: Personally roasting pahoṁka — direct harm to seed-life.
Sacitta until rendered by fire; eating it raw is a lapse.
Kept food breeds life past its time.

wordByWord.gloss: may have used curd kept beyond sixteen pahor (≈48 hrs)
wordByWord.etymology: soḷa 'sixteen' + pahora (a watch ≈ 3 hrs) + dahīṁ 'curd' + vāparavuṁ 'use'.
wordByWord.notes: Curd becomes sacitta (life-bearing) after the prescribed limit.

wordByWord.gloss: may have consumed insect-infested flowers and fruit
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saṁsakta 'infested, clinging with life' + phūla/phala + upajīvavuṁ 'live on'.
wordByWord.notes: Produce visibly holding moving life.
Unstrained water swarms with ap-kāya and creatures.
Standard catch-all that closes each block.
Closes: '…against this seventh bhogopabhoga vow.' The implicit refrain 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' follows.

literalTranslation: Seventh, the enjoyment-and-use vow is two-fold: as to food and as to occupation. Therein, as to food, in this vow: the five udumbara figs, the four great vikṛtis, ice, poison, hail, and all clay; and night-eating, many-seeded fruits, the infinite-bodied (vegetables) and pickles (1). Buttermilk-soaked vaḍā, brinjal, flowers and fruits of unknown name, pithless fruit, food whose flavour has turned — shun these twenty-two inedibles (2). These are the twenty-two abhakṣya. All tuber kinds: elephant-foot yam and vajra-tuber; fresh turmeric, fresh ginger and fresh zedoary (1); śatāvarī, vidārī, aloe, thor, giloy, garlic, bamboo-shoots, carrot, luṇī and loḍha (2); girikarṇikā, tender leaf-sprouts, the milky-sap plant, thega, fresh nut-grass, loṇaka, tree-bark, khilluhaḍa and amṛta-creeper (3); radish, ground-fungi, sprouts, ḍhaṅka, the first growth of bathuā, sūkara-creeper, spinach, tender bilva-shoots (4); yam and piṇḍālu — knowing these to be the thirty-two (anantakāyas), let the wise renounce them with effort (5). Excepting illness, famine and the like, I, to the best of my ability, renounce these thirty-two infinite-bodied vegetables. I now scrutinise the five transgressions of this seventh enjoyment-and-use (food) vow: eating living food; eating food bound to a living thing; eating half-cooked food; eating ill-cooked food; eating pithless food. Having taken a rule limiting living food, I may unknowingly have taken sacitta in excess. As for sacitta-bound: I may have put rāyaṇ pulp in my mouth and removed the stone, or scraped tree-gum and eaten it at once thinking it lifeless. As for half-cooked: I may have eaten the raw grain inside a flatbread. As for ill-cooked: I may have eaten roasted olā or pohã thinking them lifeless. As for pithless: I may have eaten tender tamarind, tender vāgaraḍā, blossom-clusters, leaf-buds, tiny unripe mango. And I may have eaten at night; eaten at an uncertain twilight hour; sat to eat in the dark; drunk water in a careless gulp. I may not have reflected, for day and for night, on the fourteen rules ('sacitta, dravya, vigai…'). In the rainy season I may have eaten old dry coconut, dried dates, dates; taken jujube, jambu, pīlu, whole hyacinth-bean pods; made parched grain with my own hand; used raw salt, stale vaḍā, stale flatbread, curd kept beyond sixteen pahor; lived on insect-infested flowers and fruit; drunk unfiltered water. And whatever else (I did) against this seventh enjoyment-and-use vow.
idiomaticTranslation: The seventh vow governs what I enjoy and what I use, and it has two faces — food and livelihood. Taking up the food side first: in this vow I am to shun the five fig-fruits, the four most degrading rich foods, ice, poison and hail, all clay, eating after dark, many-seeded fruits, the infinite-souled vegetables and aged pickles; buttermilk-vaḍā and brinjal, any flower or fruit whose name I cannot tell, pithless fruit and food gone sour — these twenty-two forbidden things. So too the thirty-two infinite-bodied roots: every kind of tuber — elephant-foot yam, vajra-root, fresh turmeric, ginger and zedoary; śatāvarī, vidārī, aloe, spurge, giloy, garlic, bamboo-shoots, carrot, luṇī, loḍha; girikarṇikā, fresh leaf-buds, the milky-sap herb, thega, fresh nut-grass, loṇaka, tree-bark, khilluhaḍa, amṛta-creeper; radish, ground-mushrooms, all sprouts, ḍhaṅka, the first shoot of bathuā, sūkara-creeper, spinach, tender bilva-buds; yam and piṇḍālu. Knowing them for what they are, the discerning should renounce them with care — and so, illness or famine aside, I renounce them as best I can. Now I cleanse the five lapses of this vow: I may have eaten living matter, or matter still joined to a living thing — sucking rāyaṇ pulp off the stone, or eating fresh tree-gum as though it were dead; I may have eaten the raw heart of a half-baked bread, or parched ola and pohã imagining them lifeless; I may have eaten unripe tamarind, tender pods, blossoms, buds, a tiny green mango. I may have eaten at night, or in the doubtful dusk, or in the dark where I could not look; gulped water heedlessly; failed to settle my fourteen daily rules for day and night. In the rains I may have eaten old coconut, dried dates and dates, jujube, jambu, pīlu, whole bean-pods; roasted grain with my own hand; used raw salt, day-old vaḍā and bread, curd past its limit; fed on insect-ridden flowers and fruit; drunk unstrained water. For these and every other slip against this seventh vow — may that misdeed of mine come to nothing.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is the longest single confession in the whole atiyāra because the seventh vow is where the householder's daily ahiṁsā is most relentlessly tested — at the plate. The verse first announces the vow's two faces (bhojanataḥ / karmataḥ) and then commits this block entirely to food, deferring livelihood to verse 8. Structurally it nests three canonical inventories inside one vow: the twenty-two abhakṣya (gāthās 1–2), the thirty-two anantakāya (gāthās 1–5), and only then the five atichāras. The logic is graded: abhakṣya are foods abandoned outright; anantakāya are a sub-class of plant whose every specimen houses innumerable one-sensed souls (so a single morsel is mass killing); the five atichāras are the subtler slips that survive even after one has 'given up' such foods. Notice how the prose deliberately glosses each Prakrit atichāra-name with a homely Gujarati example — appoṣadhi becomes 'the raw grain inside a half-baked polī', tucchoṣadhi becomes 'a tiny unripe mango' — so that an abstract canonical term lands as a concrete kitchen scruple. Two refrains do the heavy doctrinal work. 'Acitta-buddhe' (thinking it lifeless) names the recurring error: the fault is eating still-sentient matter under the mistaken belief that it has been rendered acitta. And the seasonal clause — old coconut, dates, curd-past-sixteen-pahor, unfiltered water 'in the rains' — encodes the Jain principle that a food's status is not fixed but changes with time and weather as it becomes a breeding-ground for life. The verse's own escape-valve, 'yathāśakte, excepting illness and famine', is the householder's jayaṇā: it admits that perfect abstention is a monastic ideal, and asks instead for honest, capacity-bound restraint reviewed by mind, speech and body.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on Jain bio-classification: the living world graded by senses, with vegetation as ekendriya (one-sensed) life. Anantakāya ('infinite-bodied') is the gravest vegetal category — sādhāraṇa-vanaspati, plants in which countless souls share one body, so harming one harms innumerable; this is why tubers, sprouts, fungi and fresh rhizomes are singled out while their dried or fire-rendered forms may be permitted. The sacitta/acitta distinction is the operative axis: matter is sacitta (soul-bearing) until rendered acitta by prescribed means (fire, sufficient time, drying), and the five atichāras all turn on eating sacitta or near-sacitta matter — directly, while bound to its source, half-rendered, ill-rendered, or trivially-rendered with disproportionate destruction. Rātri-bhojana is condemned both as ahiṁsā (lamps draw and darkness hides minute creatures) and as discipline. The whole list is an applied commentary on the first aṇuvrata, prāṇātipāta-viramaṇa, showing that non-violence for a layperson is finally adjudicated in the kitchen.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For a modern reciter this verse converts diet into daily mindfulness: check whether food is sacitta or properly rendered; avoid the named abhakṣya and root-vegetables to one's sincere capacity; do not eat after dark or in conditions where one cannot inspect the food; filter drinking water; respect time-limits on curd, soaked and stale items, especially in humid weather; and each day actually resolve the fourteen niyamas rather than leaving them vague. Crucially, the 'yathāśakte' clause licenses honesty over pretence — better a truthfully limited vow kept than an absolute one broken — and the closing micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ asks the reciter to own even the unknowing slip.

notes: (1) The Prakrit gāthās are preserved verbatim from the book, including non-normalised forms (e.g. mahaviga-i, rāibhoyaṇāgaṁ, lhasaṇa). (2) Several anantakāya item-names are obscure regional/Prakrit plant-words whose precise botanical identity is not securely attested — flagged here: vajjakando, luṇāo, loḍho, girikaṇiṇa, khīrisuā, thega, loṇa, khīlluhaḍo, ḍhakka, sūara-vallo. Glosses for these follow the most common traditional identifications but should be treated as approximate; the canonical count 'thirty-two' is itself partly schematic. (3) 'gulale pāṇī pīdhuṁ' (line 36–37) is read as careless/gulped drinking; the exact sense of 'gulale' is uncertain. (4) The five atichāra names appear in the book in their Prakrit forms (appoṣahi/duppoṣahi/tucchoṣahi = apakva-/duṣpakva-/tuccha-auṣadhi); the Gujarati prose supplies the operative glosses adopted here. (5) The implicit closing refrain 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' is not printed in this excerpt but is the established block-closer (preface recurringImagery (c)); noted, not invented. (6) Web verification covered the five canonical atichāras and the general abhakṣya/anantakāya framework; deep per-item botanical identifications were not independently re-verified for the obscure names listed in (2).
